Q1: I had to be really strategic about my study routine because I'm working part-time and money is tight, so I can't afford to waste time. I usually blocked out chunks of time on weekends and two evenings during the week, around 26 hours total. I'd make lists of what needed to get done and prioritize assignments over just reading. Being organized was survival for me honestly - I couldn't wing it like some students with more flexibility.
Q2: My pattern definitely shifted around deadlines. The core study times stayed pretty consistent because I had to work around my job schedule, but when big assignments were due, I'd cut into sleep or skip social things to get everything done. Some weeks were heavier than others depending on what was coming up. I learned to look ahead in the syllabus so I wouldn't get blindsided.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment details or deadlines, or when I wanted to download materials to study offline later. Sometimes I'd pop in to see if there were discussion posts I should respond to since I tried to stay active in those. Honestly, it became a habit to check in every couple of days just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became pretty automatic for me, like checking email. I think it happened because early in the semester I almost missed an announcement about a deadline change, and that scared me enough to make regular logins a habit. I'd usually check it in the morning before work and then again in the evening. It just became part of my daily routine alongside everything else.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually really valuable for me because hearing other students' perspectives helped concepts click in ways the readings alone didn't. I also found the video lectures useful - I watched a lot of them, probably over 160 minutes worth. Being able to pause and rewind helped because I could go at my own pace and really absorb the material even when I was tired after work.
Q6: Some of the supplementary readings I'd skip or just skim because I didn't have time for everything. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it often got pushed to the back burner. I'm not proud of it, but when you're stretched thin on resources and time, you have to make choices about what's essential versus nice-to-have.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to either my grade or my actual understanding of core concepts. If it helped me complete an assignment better or prepared me for the exam, I was all in. I also valued activities that let me interact with other students because that peer learning really reinforced things for me and made the course feel less isolating.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I usually couldn't afford. Required ones got done no matter what because my grade depended on them. With optional stuff, I'd evaluate whether I had the bandwidth and whether it would actually help me. Sometimes I'd feel a bit bored with required activities that seemed like busywork, but I'd push through anyway because that's what you do.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings to my laptop so I could study on the bus to work or during my lunch break when I didn't have reliable internet. I also made handwritten notes from the materials because writing things out helps me remember them better. A lot of my real learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my printed notes or re-reading downloaded materials for hours, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also do practice problems in a notebook or quiz myself using flashcards I made. The LMS only saw me when I was actively online, but my studying extended way beyond those moments.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, maybe take some notes, then download the transcript or slides and review them multiple times offline over the following days. Same with readings - I'd access them once to download, then study from the saved file. It was more practical for my situation and honestly easier on my eyes than staring at a screen constantly.
Q12: There were definitely moments when I felt bored and disengaged, especially during the middle of the semester when the initial excitement wore off but finals still felt far away. Sometimes the material just didn't grab me, and I had to push through on discipline alone. My job got busier during certain periods too, which made it harder to muster enthusiasm for coursework.
Q13: My work schedule definitely impacted when and how I used the LMS. When I had extra shifts, I'd fall behind and then have to catch up in bursts. Fatigue was real - after a long day, sometimes I'd log in with good intentions but just couldn't focus properly. I learned to do the harder cognitive work when I was fresher and save easier tasks for tired evenings.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was elsewhere, worrying about bills or just mentally exhausted. I'd realize I'd read the same paragraph three times without absorbing anything. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'engaged' with the LMS. Quality of engagement varied a lot depending on my mental state.
Q15: I'd sometimes put off discussion posts even though I knew participating mattered. It wasn't that I didn't care - I just needed to prioritize things with harder deadlines first. Occasionally I'd avoid starting a big assignment because the scope felt overwhelming, but I'd eventually break it into smaller pieces and tackle it. Procrastination happened but I always came back to things.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external structure. I'd map them all out at the start of the semester and work backward to figure out when I needed to start things. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates because that's when I was uploading work and doing final checks. Without deadlines, I honestly might have drifted more given how busy life gets.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to engage more when it was constructive and specific. If I got good marks with helpful comments, I felt energized to keep putting in effort. When feedback was delayed or generic, it was harder to feel connected to the course. I appreciated knowing what I was doing right and where I could improve - it made the work feel worthwhile.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to organize my chaotic life around. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my weeks. The discussion requirements pushed me to engage with peers, which I actually ended up valuing a lot. A looser structure might have been harder for me because I needed those external markers to keep me on track.
Q19: The video lectures being available to watch anytime was huge for me - I couldn't always attend live sessions, so that flexibility was essential. The discussion forums being asynchronous also helped because I could contribute when I had time to think. What made it harder was when multiple things were due at once - better spacing would have helped manage the workload.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information, trying to understand what's expected, or maybe feeling anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. They could also be struggling with the material and spending time trying to figure it out without producing finished work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity - sometimes it's spinning wheels.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My attendance was really low but I still passed because I was doing serious work offline. I'd download everything, study independently, and complete my assignments. The LMS didn't capture my actual study hours or the depth of my engagement with materials. Performance isn't always visible in login stats.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, what you click on, and whether you submit things on time. But they miss the quality of engagement - whether you're actually thinking deeply or just going through motions. They can't see offline studying, the conversations with classmates outside the platform, or the mental effort you're putting into understanding concepts.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively trying to understand and apply what I'm learning, not just checking boxes. It's when I'm asking myself questions about the material, connecting it to things I already know, and participating in discussions because I'm genuinely curious. It's a mental investment, not just time spent. Even when I'm bored, pushing through and doing the work is a form of engagement.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied on the bus with downloaded materials, or the study group chats I had with classmates on WhatsApp. It wouldn't capture the CBT session I did that helped me manage stress and stay resilient through tough weeks. It also wouldn't show my determination to succeed despite having limited resources - that internal drive doesn't leave digital footprints but it's what kept me going.
